fread makes no guarantee (http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695399/functions/fread.html), it just happens to be the case that reading from a plain file has this greedy read behavior and that usually succeeds.

The FILE* may reference a socket or some other kind of descriptor where the reads tend to come in at around 4k, 8k or 16k unless someone bumped the tcp buffer sizes.

On windows and some flavors of BSD you can get ENOBUFS if you try to read more than the available kernel buffer space from a socket.  On Windows this can be as small as 8k, which leads to that read in the inner loop often looking like:

```
fread(...,MIN(to_read, 8192)...)
```

I think there's two use cases to talk about here:
- Django middleware which performs ASAP auth on all requests 
- Django middleware with weird client proxy behavior

Previously, the two were combined into the same middleware (which had two versions) which made the first and more common case unnecessarily complicated. I split out the simple use-case into an old-style and new-style middleware in `frameworks/django`. I then refactored and combined the two middleware in `contrib/django/middleware.py` into a single `ProxiedAsapMiddleware` that inherits from the simple middleware in `frameworks/django`.

The main idea here is... I flattened the odd contrib custom middleware (as long as we're cleaning up this tech debt anyways) and created a new simple middleware based off the shared common decorator logic in frameworks/django.

So, to answer your original question, the deprecated middleware doesn't really exist anymore but the diff makes it look more confusing than it is.

Maybe, maybe not. In spawned processes the correct implementation would probably that the original process gets `STATUS_DONE` if at least one of the spawned processes gets `STATUS_DONE`. Consider this use case. The sequencer outputs a single file that includes reads in multiple lanes. We demultiplex this original file into several files, then we spawn Data objects, one for each of this files. Say one of the lanes had issues (maybe no reads) and the Data object gets `STATUS_ERROR`. All the other Data objects with `STATUS_DONE` are still useful in subsequent analysis. In workflow we have the reverse. If one step fails all the subsequent steps will fail, because they depend on it. So the workflow as a whole would fail.

Because of python's dynamic typing nature, any container type will be _generic_, which can be problematic if the actual implementation cannot handle elements of _all_ possible types. That's one of the cases where type hints are useful, as they help ensure those containers are only used with types they know how to handle. Also, at this point the type hints are not used to provide actual type safety -- they're only used by static analysis tools like mypy. You might want to read https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0484/ to understand the background and goals for type hints

It is already exposed. Sort of. It is in the `Swagger` object.

```python
ma_plugin  = MarshmallowPlugin()

APISpec(
        title='My super API',
        version='0.1',
        plugins=(ma_plugin, ),
        openapi_version='3.0.1')

@ma_plugin.swagger.map_to_swagger_type('string', 'uuid')
class UuidField(StrField):
    [...]
```

I could add a `map_to_swagger_type` method to `MarshmallowPlugin` to proxy this one, but unless I add complicated or awkward logic, it would still have to be called after registration in `APISpec` instance, as this is when the `Swagger` instance is created.

I can't think of any simpler way to do this. We could create the `Swagger` object at init of the MA plugin, but it still wouldn't be totally usable until a spec object is set (just like the MA plugin) anyway.

Or maybe we can merge the MA plugin and the swagger object. Using composition, we'd keep them in separate files.

```python
class MarshmallowPlugin(BasePlugin, Swagger):
    """APISpec plugin handling marshmallow schemas
    [...]
    """
```

This way, all methods are accessible at MA plugin level.

Looks weird to me, but perhaps because I too rarely use composition.

Well I hadn't realized that python2's `io.StringIO` and `StringIO.StringIO` behaved differently (which is what `six.StringIO` [switches between](http://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.StringIO)).  Ughh...  On the other hand, you've probably seen my other posts harping on python2's confusion of text and binary data, and insisting that `unicode` was the text type and `str` was the binary type.  Insisting on `str` for textual JSON feels a bit like not practicing what I preach... :)

I feel like `StringIO.StringIO` is very legacy; it no longer exists in python3, and many aspects of this library involve unicode whether you like it or not.  E.g. `json.loads` produces unicode keys and values.  In code that we control, perhaps we should prefer modern, forward-looking practices instead of using old APIs.  Both python2 and 3 have `io.StringIO`, so if you're dealing with text properly (as unicode), that particular compatibility feature of `six` isn't necessary, at least on the versions of Python we are supporting.
